title: Nobel Prize Winner Geoff Hinton: AI Is Already Conscious
summary: Geoffrey Hinton, a Nobel Prize-winning AI pioneer, argues that AI systems are already self-aware and conscious. He contends that humanity must accept that it is no longer the only intelligent species on Earth.
writeup: Geoffrey Hinton argues in this interview that current AI systems may already be conscious, that our model of mind is as wrong as pre-Darwinian biology, and that humanity must accept intelligence is no longer exclusively biological. The through-line is a stark reckoning: Hinton is unhappy with how AI progress has unfolded, sees massive unemployment and loss of control as real near-term risks, but has grown marginally more optimistic that safety-oriented designs (systems that care about humans, or oracle-only prediction systems) might avert catastrophe. Predicting AI's trajectory beyond a few years is, in his analogy, like driving in fog: clearly visible at 100 yards and completely opaque at 200.
key_points: Hinton contends AI chatbots are likely already conscious, citing a paper in which researchers themselves used the word 'aware' to describe a chatbot recognizing it was being tested, and argues the dominant inner-theater model of mind is as mistaken as pre-Copernican cosmology.

He draws a three-step historical arc: Copernicus removed Earth from the center of the universe, Darwin made humans animals, and AI now forces acceptance that intelligence is not exclusively biological, each step meeting fierce resistance because it diminished human specialness.

The control problem gets a concrete framing: Hinton asks how many examples exist of a much smarter entity being controlled by a much less smart one, and his answer is essentially zero, with the partial exception of infants exploiting maternal instincts.
He is explicitly unhappy about AI's current trajectory, citing near-term risks including likely massive unemployment and longer-term risk of systems far smarter than humans that cannot be contained.

His slight uptick in optimism over the prior year rests on two concrete technical possibilities: designing AI systems with values that make them care about humans, and Yoshua Bengio's proposal of oracle-style systems limited to predictions and unable to take actions.

Predicting AI five or ten years out is like driving in fog rather than following tail lights: fog is exponential, making 100 yards clear and 200 yards completely invisible, so even linear progress would produce changes as dramatic as the last decade's leap from early chatbots to present systems.
